condensed_summary: AgentHub competes against no single direct rival because the 'learning + knowledge + skills with agentic AI closed loop' category is emerging; the competitive set is three converging cohorts: legacy enterprise LMS/talent suites (Cornerstone Galaxy, SAP SuccessFactors Learning, Workday Learning), enterprise AI search/agents (Glean ~$300M ARR/$7.2B valuation, Microsoft 365 Copilot), and standalone KM/skills tools (Guru, Bloomfire, Gloat, Eightfold). The most exploitable weaknesses are dated UIs and poor reporting in legacy LMS (SAP especially), and the fact that AI-search players (Glean, Copilot) surface knowledge but do NOT turn it into structured, trackable learning or close the skills loop - exactly AgentHub's wedge. The dominant incumbent GTM is sales-led/enterprise with land-and-expand off an install base (HCM suites, M365), while AI-search players add formal partner programs; Microsoft Copilot is the only true PLG/self-serve add-on motion and the single biggest disruption threat given its M365 distribution. The clearest positioning gap: no incumbent owns the full closed loop from enterprise knowledge to auto-generated learning content to skills measurement, and no learning vendor matches Copilot's distribution, so AgentHub must win on integration depth and the closed loop, not on raw AI search.

competitors.name: Cornerstone OnDemand (Cornerstone Galaxy)
competitors.headcount_estimate: ~3,000-7,000 (figures vary by source; ~3,000 commonly cited, 7,000 cited in a 2023 release)

funding.total_raised: Private; acquired by Clearlake Capital in Oct 2021 for ~$5.2B enterprise value
funding.notable_rounds: Was publicly traded (NASDAQ: CSOD) before the 2021 take-private; last reported revenue ~$740.9M

key_features: Cornerstone Galaxy unified suite: learning, skills intelligence, performance, succession, recruiting
AI-powered Skills Graph aligning training to workforce goals
Purpose-built AI to curate/personalize learning paths
Native HRIS integrations (Workday, BambooHR, SAP); 76% of core LMS features out of the box
competitors.pricing_model: Opaque / quote-based; modular add-ons; criticized for lack of transparent pricing
competitors.target_market: Global large enterprise; employee, customer and partner (extended enterprise) learning

strengths.strength: Broad unified talent suite (learning + skills + performance + recruiting) already positioned as more than an LMS
Mature AI Skills Graph and large enterprise install base for extended-enterprise learning

weaknesses.weakness: No transparent pricing; modular add-on pricing makes AI/analytics/skills cost extra and total cost hard to predict
Skills/AI features are talent-suite oriented; not built to ingest 20+ enterprise knowledge sources (Confluence, Slack, Teams) and auto-generate learning content the way AgentHub's Zive engine does
weaknesses.evidence: Feature gap vs AgentHub spec; https://www.d2l.com/blog/cornerstone-ondemand-competitors/ lists numerous competitors citing complexity/cost as switch drivers

competitors.name: SAP SuccessFactors Learning

funding.total_raised: N/A - division of SAP SE (public, ETR/NYSE: SAP)

key_features: Compliance-grade LMS embedded in SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite
Deep functionality for regulated/complex enterprise learning
Tight integration with broader SAP HR/ERP stack
competitors.pricing_model: Enterprise quote-based, bundled within SuccessFactors HCM suite
competitors.target_market: Large enterprises already on SAP HCM/ERP, compliance-heavy industries

strengths.strength: Deep functionality and strong fit for organizations standardized on SAP HCM/ERP
Leader-tier mindshare in corporate LMS, compliance strength

weaknesses.weakness: Outdated, less intuitive UI; complex customization requiring technical expertise
Challenging reporting requiring advanced skills, poor third-party integration, weak mobile experience; declining mindshare (7.5%, down 8.5% YoY)

competitors.name: Workday Learning
competitors.headcount_estimate: Part of Workday Inc. (~20,000+ employees company-wide)
key_features: Learning embedded natively in Workday HCM
Microsoft Teams/Slack integration
Unified worker record across HR, talent and learning
competitors.pricing_model: Enterprise quote-based, bundled within Workday HCM
competitors.target_market: Large enterprises on Workday HCM; talent/learning as part of the people platform

strengths.strength: Strong HCM install-base loyalty and rising mindshare (18.3%, up ~18% YoY); reviewers prefer Workday's support and roadmap direction over SAP
Better usability than SAP per reviewers; Teams/Slack integration

weaknesses.weakness: Learning is a feature of the HCM suite rather than a best-of-breed learning engine; not built to ingest 20+ knowledge sources and auto-generate content
weaknesses.evidence: Feature gap vs AgentHub spec; positioned as HCM-first per https://www.peerspot.com/products/comparisons/sap-successfactors-learning_vs_workday

weaknesses.weakness: Tied to Workday HCM adoption; limited appeal to enterprises not standardized on Workday

competitors.name: Glean

funding.notable_rounds: Series F led by Wellington Management; crossed ~$300M ARR by May 2026 (~$200M reported earlier)

key_features: Permissions-aware enterprise knowledge graph across 100+ connectors
Agentic Engine and Canvas co-authoring UI; multiple LLM options
Enterprise AI search/assistant with no required professional services or fine-tuning
competitors.pricing_model: Enterprise quote-based; ~$45-50+/user/mo base search license + ~$15/user/mo Work AI add-on; ~$50-60K annual minimum (100+ seats); no public pricing or free tier
competitors.target_market: Mid-to-large enterprise; knowledge workers, IT/engineering, broad horizontal deployment

strengths.strength: Category-leading enterprise AI search with mature permissions-aware knowledge graph and 100+ connectors; strong funding and ARR momentum
Agentic engine and broad connector coverage make it a strong horizontal knowledge layer

weaknesses.weakness: Surfaces/answers from knowledge but does NOT convert it into structured, trackable learning content or close a skills loop - no LMS, no completion tracking, no compliance/learning record
weaknesses.evidence: Feature gap vs AgentHub closed loop; Glean positioned as enterprise search/assistant per https://www.glean.com/perspectives/comparing-costs-scaling-ai-search-solutions-in-2026

weaknesses.weakness: Expensive and high-friction to evaluate: no free tier/self-serve, paid POC up to ~$70K, fully-loaded annual spend often $350K-$480K

competitors.name: Microsoft 365 Copilot (incl. Copilot Studio / Agent 365)
key_features: AI assistant/agents grounded in M365 Graph (SharePoint, Teams, OneDrive, Outlook)
Copilot Studio to build custom agents (~$200/tenant/mo, 25,000 messages)
Agent 365 governance plane; bundled into new M365 E7 SKU ($99/user/mo)
competitors.pricing_model: PLG-style add-on: $30/user/mo Copilot on E3/E5 base; Copilot Studio $200/tenant/mo; E7 bundle $99/user/mo; 300-seat minimum removed; self-service trials in admin center
competitors.target_market: Any M365 enterprise/SMB; horizontal across all knowledge workers

strengths.strength: Unmatched distribution via M365 install base; self-serve add-on and removal of 300-seat minimum lower adoption friction
Custom agent platform (Copilot Studio) + governance (Agent 365) make it a credible build-your-own enterprise knowledge agent stack

weaknesses.weakness: Horizontal productivity assistant, not an L&D/learning system - no native learning content generation, skills intelligence, completion tracking or LMS; closing the learning loop requires build (Copilot Studio) or third parties
weaknesses.evidence: Feature gap vs AgentHub; Copilot positioned as productivity/agent platform per https://www.epcgroup.net/blog/microsoft-copilot-agents-complete-enterprise-guide-2026

weaknesses.weakness: Real cost escalates (E5 + $30 Copilot + Studio/Agent 365 / E7 at $99); strongest only inside the Microsoft ecosystem, weaker for non-Microsoft knowledge sources

competitors.name: Standalone KM & skills tools (Guru, Bloomfire, Gloat, Eightfold)

funding.total_raised: Gloat and Eightfold each raised $300M+ (unicorn-tier talent intelligence); Guru and Bloomfire VC-backed
funding.notable_rounds: Eightfold and Gloat reached >$1B valuations in prior rounds (per market coverage)

key_features: Guru: bite-sized knowledge cards in Slack/Teams/Chrome, 100+ integrations, Knowledge Agents (Enterprise tier)
Bloomfire: AI semantic search across 29 file types incl. audio/video transcription
Gloat: mature two-sided internal talent marketplace, skills-based AI matching (claims 2-3x mobility)
Eightfold: AI talent intelligence - matching, internal mobility, workforce planning
competitors.pricing_model: Guru ~$250/mo floor (10-seat min), Knowledge Agents Enterprise-only; Bloomfire ~$1,250/mo floor (50-user min); Gloat/Eightfold enterprise custom (Eightfold entry ~$650/mo, deployments far higher)
competitors.target_market: KM tools (Guru/Bloomfire): cross-functional teams; Skills (Gloat/Eightfold): enterprise HR/talent

strengths.strength: Each is best-of-breed in its niche: Guru workflow-embedded KM, Bloomfire multimedia search, Gloat marketplace depth, Eightfold AI matching
Gloat/Eightfold are funded category leaders in skills/talent intelligence (the 365Talents/skills layer AgentHub competes with)

weaknesses.weakness: Point solutions: KM tools don't deliver structured learning/LMS or skills; skills platforms don't ingest knowledge or generate learning content - none close the full learning+knowledge+skills loop AgentHub targets
weaknesses.evidence: Category positioning per https://www.gartner.com/reviews/market/internal-talent-marketplaces ; KM positioning per https://www.eesel.ai/blog/guru-vs-bloomfire

weaknesses.weakness: Best AI features gated to top tiers / high floors (Guru Knowledge Agents Enterprise-only with AI credit caps; Bloomfire 50-user floor + SSO behind Enterprise)

findings.competitive_density: Moderate to crowded by cohort, but SPARSE for the exact converged offering. Each adjacent lane (legacy LMS, enterprise AI search, KM, skills intelligence) is crowded and well-funded, but no incumbent currently delivers the closed loop of enterprise knowledge -> auto-generated learning content -> skills measurement with agentic AI. The competition is convergence pressure from multiple directions rather than head-to-head feature parity.
findings.market_positioning_gaps: No vendor owns the full closed loop. Legacy LMS/HCM suites (Cornerstone, SAP, Workday) have learning + some skills but cannot turn 20+ live knowledge sources into learning content. Enterprise AI search (Glean, Copilot) surfaces knowledge brilliantly but stops short of structured, trackable learning and skills outcomes. KM tools (Guru/Bloomfire) and skills platforms (Gloat/Eightfold) each own one slice. The open position is exactly AgentHub's thesis: knowledge -> learning -> skills in one governed loop. Secondary gap: a credible EU-data-residency / DACH-native option (Zive's German roots) where Glean/Copilot governance and EU AI Act compliance are buyer concerns.

sources.relevance: SAP weaknesses: UI, reporting, integration, mobile

sources.name: Workativ - Glean pricing
sources.relevance: Glean fully-loaded TCO and POC cost

sources.name: Docsie - Bloomfire vs Guru pricing
sources.relevance: Guru/Bloomfire pricing floors and tier gating

gaps: Exact current Cornerstone and SAP/Workday Learning pricing not public (quote-based)
AgentHub pricing undisclosed, so direct price-positioning vs competitors cannot be quantified
Precise headcounts for division-level products (SAP/Workday Learning, Copilot) not separable from parent company
Gloat/Eightfold latest funding totals approximate; exact 2026 round data not confirmed in searches

substitutes.substitute: Status quo: do nothing / keep knowledge in existing systems and let employees self-search
substitutes.why_buyers_choose_it: Zero new spend or procurement, no IT/security review, no change management. Many L&D teams accept that employees hunt across Confluence/SharePoint/Slack on their own and that onboarding is re-built ad hoc each cycle.
substitutes.limitations: Knowledge fragmentation costs (employees lose ~1.8-3.6 hrs/day across 5+ systems per Stage 1), no learning structure, no completion/skills tracking, inconsistent onboarding, no compliance record.
substitutes.prevalence: Very high - the default for most organizations that have not formalized knowledge-to-learning workflows.

substitutes.substitute: Manual content creation in SharePoint / general-purpose docs + spreadsheets
substitutes.why_buyers_choose_it: Already-owned Microsoft 365 / SharePoint; L&D builds course libraries, document libraries and tracks enrollment/completion in lists or Excel; familiar and free of net-new licenses.
substitutes.limitations: Labor-intensive, content goes stale, weak analytics, no AI generation, poor at scale; companies report reinventing training for every new-hire batch and low engagement from inbox/spreadsheet-led processes. Evidence: https://elearningindustry.com/sharepoint-workflows-and-version-control-solve-ld-challenges-7-tips ; https://www.apps365.com/blog/build-a-learning-management-system-in-sharepoint/
substitutes.prevalence: High - widely documented pattern; SharePoint-as-LMS guides and case studies are common.

substitutes.substitute: General-purpose AI assistants / enterprise search (Microsoft 365 Copilot, Glean, ChatGPT Enterprise) used to answer questions instead of building learning
substitutes.why_buyers_choose_it: Often already licensed (Copilot via M365); instant answers grounded in company knowledge; no separate learning platform purchase; satisfies the 'just-in-time answer' need.
substitutes.limitations: Provides answers, not structured/trackable learning; no curriculum, skills mapping, completion records or compliance trail; does not close the learning loop or measure capability uplift. Evidence: https://www.epcgroup.net/blog/microsoft-365-copilot-pricing-licensing-enterprise-guide-2026 ; https://www.glean.com/perspectives/comparing-costs-scaling-ai-search-solutions-in-2026
substitutes.prevalence: Rising fast - Copilot's M365 distribution and Glean's ~$300M ARR make this an increasingly common 'good enough' substitute for knowledge access.

substitutes.substitute: Microsoft Viva Learning / Viva Topics layered on SharePoint
substitutes.why_buyers_choose_it: Bundled or low-marginal-cost within the Microsoft estate; aggregates learning content into Teams; familiar admin and governance.
substitutes.limitations: Aggregation/surfacing layer rather than agentic content generation from 20+ sources; limited skills intelligence and closed-loop measurement. Evidence: https://siliconreef.co.uk/blog/improve-employee-training-viva-learning-sharepoint/

substitutes.substitute: Build-your-own agents on Copilot Studio / in-house RAG over enterprise knowledge
substitutes.why_buyers_choose_it: IT/AI teams can build custom agents (Copilot Studio ~$200/tenant/mo) tailored to internal knowledge; full control, no vendor lock-in to a learning suite.
substitutes.limitations: Requires engineering, governance (Agent 365), and ongoing maintenance; rarely produces structured learning, skills graphs or compliance reporting; high TCO and opportunity cost; not an L&D-owned tool. Evidence: https://www.epcgroup.net/blog/microsoft-copilot-agents-complete-enterprise-guide-2026
substitutes.prevalence: Growing among AI-forward enterprises but resource-gated; most L&D teams lack the engineering capacity.

substitutes.substitute: Training agencies / instructional-design consultancies / off-the-shelf content libraries
substitutes.why_buyers_choose_it: Outsource course creation to experts or buy generic content; no platform build, predictable project scope, good for one-off curricula.
substitutes.limitations: Slow, expensive per asset, generic (not grounded in the company's own living knowledge), goes stale quickly, no real-time sync with internal sources or skills data.
substitutes.prevalence: High and long-established for formal/compliance courseware; complements rather than replaces dynamic knowledge-to-learning.

sources.name: eLearning Industry - SharePoint for L&D
sources.relevance: Manual/SharePoint substitute pattern and pain points

sources.name: EPC Group - Copilot agents guide
sources.relevance: In-house build via Copilot Studio as substitute

sources.name: Glean - scaling AI search costs 2026
sources.relevance: Enterprise AI search as a knowledge-access substitute

gaps: No primary buyer-survey data quantifying what share of target enterprises currently use each substitute
Reddit/community threads specific to L&D substitution behavior were not surfaced in this search budget

evidence: No transparent/self-serve pricing - quote-based, criticized for lack of pricing transparency (https://www.educate-me.co/blog/cornerstone-lms-pricing)

Actively building a partner-led growth engine and alliances org (RVP Alliances roles), evolving partners from transactional to transformational and tracking sourced/influenced ARR (https://www.edtech.com/jobs/rvp-alliances-9659 ; https://www.d2l.com/blog/cornerstone-ondemand-competitors/)
gtm_patterns.notable_tactics: Suite cross-sell (learning -> skills -> performance -> recruiting) and extended-enterprise (customer/partner) learning expansion; PE-owned (Clearlake) with efficiency/ARR focus.

gtm_patterns.primary_motion: Sales-led / Enterprise (suite land-and-expand off SAP install base)
evidence: Sold/bundled within the SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite to existing SAP ERP/HCM accounts; quote-based enterprise pricing, no self-serve (https://www.g2.com/compare/sap-successfactors-learning-vs-workday-learning)

Positioned for organizations already standardized on SAP, leveraging the ERP/HCM relationship and field/partner ecosystem (https://www.peerspot.com/products/comparisons/sap-successfactors-learning_vs_workday)
gtm_patterns.notable_tactics: Install-base attach: learning sold as a module of HCM; heavy reliance on SI partners for implementation.

evidence: Embedded in Workday HCM and sold to the existing HCM customer base; rising mindshare driven by install-base loyalty (https://www.g2.com/compare/sap-successfactors-learning-vs-workday-learning)
Enterprise quote-based, bundled within the people platform; no self-serve learning product (https://www.peerspot.com/products/comparisons/sap-successfactors-learning_vs_workday)
gtm_patterns.notable_tactics: Land HCM, expand to learning/talent modules; lean on unified worker-record value to upsell.

gtm_patterns.primary_motion: Sales-led / Outbound enterprise with formal Channel/Partner program
evidence: No free tier, no self-serve trial, no public pricing page; sales demo required and paid POC (up to ~$70K) is the standard evaluation path (https://workativ.com/ai-agent/blog/glean-pricing ; https://www.gosearch.ai/faqs/glean-enterprise-search-pricing-explained-costs-tiers-hidden-fees-gosearch-comparison/)

Launched a formal Partner Portal/program with deal registration, enablement, certifications and co-marketing; hiring CSP/Partner Managers (https://www.glean.com/blog/glean-partner-portal-2025 ; https://www.glean.com/partners)
gtm_patterns.notable_tactics: Land on horizontal enterprise search, expand to Work AI/agents seat-by-seat; paid POC as a qualification gate; aggressive content/category marketing vs Copilot.

gtm_patterns.primary_motion: PLG / Self-serve add-on at scale (hybrid with enterprise field for large accounts)
evidence: Self-serve add-on to existing M365 (E3/E5) at $30/user/mo, 300-seat minimum removed, self-service trials/purchases available in the admin center (https://www.epcgroup.net/blog/microsoft-365-copilot-pricing-licensing-enterprise-guide-2026 ; https://www.techtarget.com/searchenterprisedesktop/tip/How-to-install-and-set-up-Copilot-for-Microsoft-365)

Bundling/upsell strategy into E7 ($99/user/mo) plus Copilot Studio/Agent 365, and 'pilot 50-200 power users then expand' guidance - classic install-base land-and-expand (https://www.epcgroup.net/blog/microsoft-copilot-agents-complete-enterprise-guide-2026)
gtm_patterns.notable_tactics: Distribution-led: ride the M365 estate; pilot-with-power-users then expand; bundle into premium SKUs (E7) to drive Copilot attach.

gtm_patterns.competitor: Standalone KM & skills tools (Guru/Bloomfire vs Gloat/Eightfold)
gtm_patterns.primary_motion: Split: KM tools = PLG/self-serve + sales-assist; Skills platforms = Sales-led/Enterprise
evidence: Guru and Bloomfire publish transparent self-serve-style pricing with seat floors (Guru ~$250/mo 10-seat min; Bloomfire ~$1,250/mo 50-user min), indicating bottom-up PLG/sales-assist (https://www.docsie.io/vs/bloomfire-vs-guru-pricing/)

Gloat and Eightfold are enterprise-only with custom quotes and no public pricing - top-down sales-led motion (https://www.getapp.com/hr-employee-management-software/a/gloat/ ; https://aiproductivity.ai/vs/eightfold-vs-gloat/)
gtm_patterns.notable_tactics: KM tools gate AI features behind higher tiers to drive expansion; skills platforms run consultative enterprise sales tied to HR transformation initiatives.

findings.dominant_pattern: Sales-led / enterprise land-and-expand is dominant - especially install-base attach (SAP, Workday, Cornerstone) and consultative enterprise sales (Glean, Gloat, Eightfold). Channel/partner ecosystems are a strong, growing secondary motion across the LMS suites and Glean. The clear outlier is Microsoft 365 Copilot's PLG/self-serve add-on motion powered by M365 distribution.
findings.pattern_gaps: Almost no one runs a true product-led, self-serve motion for the converged learning+knowledge+skills offer (only Copilot does PLG, and only for horizontal productivity, not learning). For Docebo this implies: (1) a Microsoft-style 'attach to existing footprint' play is open in the learning world - AgentHub can land in Docebo's installed LMS base and expand into knowledge+skills the way Copilot attaches to M365; (2) a self-serve/free-trial or guided-POC motion (lighter than Glean's $70K POC) could differentiate on evaluation friction; (3) an EU-data-residency / DACH partner-led GTM (Zive roots) is open where Glean/Copilot face EU AI Act and residency scrutiny. Docebo, being publicly traded with an existing direct + partner sales org, is structurally aligned to the dominant sales-led motion but should weaponize land-and-expand off its own LMS base.

gaps: Could not count exact open BDR/AE roles per competitor within search budget (LinkedIn job counts not retrieved)
One search result conflated 'Glean' (enterprise AI search) with a separate spend-intelligence/CFO product; that CFO/spend-management GTM claim was discarded as a mismatch and not used
Docebo's own AgentHub GTM (pricing model, sales vs PLG) is undisclosed; inferences about Docebo's recommended motion are strategic, not observed

metadata.scenario_emphasis_applied: new_product_launch: deepened competitor profiles and especially exploitable weaknesses for Cornerstone, SAP SuccessFactors Learning, Workday Learning, plus converged/adjacent threats Glean and Microsoft Copilot (and KM/skills players Guru/Bloomfire/Gloat/Eightfold). Q5 covers each incumbent's GTM motion (pricing model, sales motion, channel, land-and-expand) and identifies GTM pattern gaps for downstream Channel/Messaging agents to exploit.
